The tribes did not complain to Rehoboam about his father's idolatry or rebellion against God. What was their greatest concern was nothing to them; they were so careless about religion if they could live comfortably and pay no taxes. People who cause trouble will always find something to complain about. And when we see the accounts in Scripture of Solomon’s reign, the peace, wealth, and prosperity Israel then enjoyed, we cannot doubt that their complaints were false or exaggerated. Rehoboam answered the people according to the advice of the young men. No one was more blinded by pride and a desire for absolute power than he was; nothing is more dangerous. God's plans were fulfilled in this way. He left Rehoboam to his own foolishness, and hid from him things that would have brought him peace, so that the kingdom could be taken away from him. God accomplishes his wise and righteous purposes through the mistakes and sins of people. Those who lose heaven throw it away, as Rehoboam did, by being stubborn and foolish.

The people spoke disrespectfully about David. How quickly good men and their good deeds for the public are forgotten! These considerations should help us accept our losses and troubles, because God is the cause of them, and our brothers were the means: let us not think about revenge. Rehoboam and his people listened to the word of the Lord. When we know what God wants, we must submit, even if it goes against what we want. If we secure God’s favor, nothing in the entire universe can harm us.

Jeroboam did not trust in God's care; he would plan ways and means, and sinful ones too, for his own safety. A practical disbelief in God's all-sufficiency is at the root of every time we turn away from him. Although it is likely that he intended his worship to be for Jehovah, the God of Israel, it was against God’s law and dishonored God’s majesty to be represented this way. The people might have been less shocked about worshipping the God of Israel with an image than if they had been asked to worship Baal right away; but it opened the door to idolatry. Blessed Lord, give us grace to respect your temple, your ordinances, your house of prayer, your sabbaths, and never again, like Jeroboam, set up any idol in our hearts. Be you everything precious to us; reign and rule in our hearts, the hope of glory.
